refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and Equipment Operator in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire volumetric or mass flow data from material handling subsystems during operation.
Calculate throughput rate (m³/h or ton/h) from acquired flow data.
Display or transmit throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in real time or near-real time.
Log historical throughput data for post-operation analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput
DP2: Periodically sample material quantity at fixed time intervals, compute differential changes, and extrapolate to period-level throughput estimate
DP3: Instrument material input and output boundaries with independent accumulation counters (mass or volume), compute net throughput as difference, and normalize by elapsed time
DP4: Establish reference material batches of known quantity, measure batch processing cycle time, and derive throughput as batch quantity divided by cycle duration
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Instrument material input and output boundaries with independent accumulation counters (mass or volume), compute net throughput as difference, and normalize by elapsed time

potential_sources: Input and output counter calibration drift (mitigated by independent sensing paths)
Time synchronization between counters and reporting system (low risk if synchronized to common clock)
Assumption that input/output boundaries are physically accessible and well-defined (worksite constraint dependency)
coupling_check.notes: DP3 exhibits low functional coupling: input measurement is logically independent of output measurement, and both are decoupled from the reporting/integration logic. This supports modular IM decomposition. DP1 risks sensor drift accumulation over long periods. DP2 introduces sampling bias and extrapolation error. DP4 is brittle if batch size or cycle time varies. DP3 is robust to continuous operational variation and supports real-time or batch reporting without loss of fidelity.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material input and output streams are physically distinct and measurable at defined boundaries. Risk: If material recirculates or boundaries are ambiguous, net throughput calculation becomes invalid. Mitigation: Validate worksite layout and operational flow during design phase.

Assumption: Accumulation counters can be installed without disrupting material flow or safety. Risk: Installation complexity or operational interference. Mitigation: Coordinate with worksite safety plan; consider non-invasive sensing alternatives if constraints prohibit direct instrumentation.

Assumption: Evaluation period is sufficiently long (relative to counter resolution) to achieve acceptable measurement precision. Risk: If period is short or material throughput is low, quantization error dominates. Mitigation: Specify minimum evaluation period and counter resolution in acceptance criteria.

Assumption: Reported productivity ≥ productivity_target is achievable under representative operations without system-induced losses. Risk: If measurement system introduces friction or delay, actual throughput may be artificially suppressed. Mitigation: Validate that IM realization (sensor placement, data latency) does not degrade operational performance.

Assumption: Time normalization (throughput = net quantity / elapsed time) is valid across all operational states. Risk: If system is idle or in transient startup/shutdown phases, averaging may mask performance issues. Mitigation: Define 'representative operations' precisely; consider stratified reporting (steady-state vs. transient).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) required measurement precision (e.g., ±5% of productivity_target), (2) temporal granularity of reporting (real-time, batch, periodic), (3) definition of 'representative operations' (steady-state duration, operational modes included), and (4) physical accessibility constraints at input/output boundaries. This will enable IM selection (e.g., load cells, flow meters, or discrete counters) and validation protocol design without ambiguity. Additionally, specify whether the system must report throughput continuously or only at evaluation period end, as this affects DP3 implementation complexity and IM module boundaries (e.g., real-time data aggregation vs. post-hoc calculation).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized material flow monitoring and throughput computation subsystem that instruments input/output boundaries, maintains independent accumulation counters, and derives net throughput metrics

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit computed net throughput, accumulation counter states, and material flow metrics to external monitoring/logging systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive configuration parameters for counter initialization, boundary definitions, and throughput normalization intervals

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signal anomalous throughput or counter inconsistencies that may indicate material loss, blockage, or sensor failure

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest cohesion: material flow instrumentation, accumulation logic, and throughput computation are core to NGHE operational monitoring. Counter state management and normalization algorithms remain internal. Minor diffusion: telemetry export and safety alerting require external interfaces, but these are well-defined and minimal.

Poor fit: Vehicle module typically owns propulsion, navigation, and structural subsystems. Material flow monitoring is orthogonal to vehicle dynamics. Significant diffusion: counter logic would leak into vehicle control loops; throughput computation would require cross-cutting dependencies on material handling subsystems outside vehicle scope.

Weak containment: Teleoperation owns remote command/control and operator interface. Material flow instrumentation is not a teleoperation concern. High diffusion: accumulation counters and throughput computation would be orphaned from their natural operational context; would require bidirectional coupling to NGHE Solution for sensor data.

Moderate but misaligned: Safety Services owns hazard detection and mitigation. While anomalous throughput can trigger safety alerts, the core instrumentation and accumulation logic are not safety-specific. Containment would be inverted: Safety Services would depend on NGHE Solution for throughput data, making Safety Services a consumer, not the owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP3 (material flow instrumentation with independent accumulation counters and net throughput computation) is most cohesively contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) material input/output boundaries are intrinsic to NGHE operational semantics; (2) accumulation counter state and normalization logic are core to NGHE monitoring and control; (3) throughput metrics directly inform NGHE task execution and efficiency assessment; (4) external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) are minimal and well-scoped, with no major algorithmic diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If material flow sensors are physically distributed across Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems, sensor data acquisition may require cross-module dependencies. Mitigation: Define a clear sensor abstraction layer within NGHE Solution that decouples physical sensor location from logical counter ownership.

Risk: Safety Services may need real-time access to throughput anomalies for hazard detection. If SafetyAlert interface becomes bidirectional or high-frequency, coupling could increase. Mitigation: Establish SafetyAlert as a unidirectional event stream from NGHE Solution to Safety Services; avoid polling or synchronous queries.

Risk: If Teleoperation requires operator visibility into live throughput, Telemetry interface may become a bottleneck or require dual publication. Mitigation: Telemetry interface should support subscription-based filtering; Teleoperation consumes Telemetry as a read-only feed, not a direct dependency.

Risk: Counter initialization and boundary configuration (via Tasking) may require coordination with Vehicle or Teleoperation if material handling is mode-dependent. Mitigation: Tasking interface should be stateless and idempotent; NGHE Solution owns the interpretation of task parameters.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria (reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period) is directly measurable via NGHE Solution's throughput computation and telemetry export. Counter states and net throughput metrics are observable and loggable. Worksite safety plan integration is supported via SafetyAlert interface. No hidden or indirect measurement dependencies.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution owns the complete FR realization: sensor abstraction, accumulation counter logic, net throughput normalization, and metric derivation. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) are well-scoped and unidirectional or event-driven, minimizing bidirectional coupling.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 (independent accumulation counters at input/output boundaries, net throughput as difference, normalized by elapsed time) is a sound and complete solution to FR (enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time). The logic is deterministic, testable, and aligns with standard material balance principles. No functional gaps or contradictions identified.

diffusion_risks: Sensor data acquisition: If material flow sensors are physically distributed across Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems, NGHE Solution must define a clear sensor abstraction layer to decouple physical location from logical counter ownership. Risk level: Medium. Mitigation: Establish a sensor interface contract (e.g., sensor adapter pattern) that Vehicle/Teleoperation implement; NGHE Solution consumes only the abstracted sensor feed.

SafetyAlert bidirectionality: If Safety Services requires real-time throughput anomaly queries or if SafetyAlert becomes high-frequency bidirectional, coupling could increase and create latency/synchronization risks. Risk level: Medium. Mitigation: Enforce SafetyAlert as a unidirectional event stream (NGHE → Safety Services); Safety Services must not poll or synchronously query NGHE Solution for throughput state.

Teleoperation operator visibility: If Teleoperation requires live throughput display, Telemetry interface may become a bottleneck or require dual publication paths. Risk level: Low-Medium. Mitigation: Telemetry interface should support subscription-based filtering and rate-limiting; Teleoperation consumes Telemetry as a read-only feed with no back-channel dependency.

Task parameter coordination: If counter initialization and boundary configuration (via Tasking) must coordinate with Vehicle or Teleoperation mode changes, coupling could increase. Risk level: Low. Mitigation: Tasking interface must be stateless and idempotent; NGHE Solution owns interpretation of task parameters and is responsible for consistency.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution exhibits high cohesion (0.92 containment score, 0.08 diffusion score). Core responsibilities (material flow instrumentation, accumulation logic, throughput computation, counter state management) are tightly coupled and belong together. External interfaces are minimal, well-defined, and mostly unidirectional. Alternative allocations (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) show poor fit (0.35–0.5 containment) and would introduce significant algorithmic diffusion and cross-cutting dependencies. NGHE Solution is the correct ownership boundary. Recommended: Formalize sensor abstraction layer and enforce interface contracts to prevent diffusion into Vehicle/Teleoperation.

recommended_revisions: Define a sensor abstraction interface (e.g., MaterialFlowSensor contract) that decouples physical sensor location from NGHE Solution's counter logic. Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems implement sensor adapters; NGHE Solution consumes only the abstracted feed. This prevents diffusion of counter logic into vehicle/teleoperation modules.

Establish SafetyAlert as a unidirectional event stream (NGHE Solution → Safety Services). Document that Safety Services must not poll or synchronously query NGHE Solution for throughput state. If real-time anomaly detection is required, Safety Services subscribes to SafetyAlert events and maintains its own derived state.

Specify Telemetry interface as a subscription-based feed with optional filtering and rate-limiting. Teleoperation and external monitoring systems consume Telemetry as read-only subscribers. No back-channel queries or synchronous dependencies.

Formalize Tasking interface as a stateless, idempotent command set (e.g., InitializeCounters, SetBoundaryDefinition, SetNormalizationInterval). Document that NGHE Solution owns interpretation and consistency; Tasking does not require coordination with Vehicle or Teleoperation state.

Add explicit acceptance test: Verify that counter state, throughput metrics, and SafetyAlert events are correctly computed and exported under representative material flow scenarios (e.g., steady-state flow, blockage, sensor failure). Confirm that reported productivity meets ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period.

Document sensor failure modes and counter inconsistency detection logic. SafetyAlert should signal not only anomalous throughput but also counter divergence, sensor drift, or missing data. This strengthens the link between DP3 and worksite safety plan requirements.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR3 is well-formed, DP3 is sound, and NGHE Solution is the correct ownership boundary. However, safety criticality (high) and identified diffusion risks (sensor abstraction, SafetyAlert bidirectionality, counter consistency detection) warrant decomposition of DP3 into lower-level sub-FRs to ensure safety-critical counter logic, sensor integration, and anomaly detection are explicitly designed and testable. Recommended revisions (sensor abstraction layer, unidirectional SafetyAlert, counter failure mode detection) are architectural concerns that must be pushed down as functional requirements on sub-modules to maintain traceability and enable independent verification. Stopping here would leave safety-critical sensor integration and failure detection as implicit design assumptions rather than explicit, verifiable requirements.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Acquire and abstract material flow sensor data (input and output boundaries) in a location-agnostic manner, decoupling physical sensor placement from counter logic.
FR3.2: Accumulate material flow counts at input and output boundaries with deterministic, consistent state management and counter initialization.
FR3.3: Compute net throughput as the difference between output and input accumulated counts, normalized by elapsed time, and express result in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
FR3.4: Detect and report counter inconsistencies, sensor failures, and anomalous throughput deviations to Safety Services via unidirectional SafetyAlert event stream.
FR3.5: Export accumulated counters, computed throughput metrics, and diagnostic state to Telemetry interface as a subscription-based, read-only feed with optional filtering and rate-limiting.
FR3.6: Accept and interpret task parameters (counter initialization, boundary definition, normalization interval) via stateless, idempotent Tasking interface commands.

revise.why: DP3 is logically sound but insufficiently detailed for safety-critical implementation. Decomposition into sub-DPs will address: (1) sensor abstraction pattern to prevent diffusion into Vehicle/Teleoperation; (2) counter state consistency and failure detection logic (critical for safety); (3) unidirectional SafetyAlert event contract; (4) Telemetry subscription model; (5) Tasking idempotency and statelessness. These sub-DPs will be derived from sub-FRs and will form the basis for detailed design and safety case evidence.

assumptions: Material flow sensors are physically distributed across Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems but can be abstracted via a common sensor interface contract (MaterialFlowSensor adapter pattern).
SafetyAlert can be implemented as a unidirectional event stream without requiring bidirectional queries or synchronous state polling from Safety Services.
Counter state consistency can be monitored and divergence detected via comparison of input/output accumulation rates and sensor health signals.
Telemetry interface supports subscription-based filtering and rate-limiting without creating bottlenecks for live operator visibility.
Tasking interface commands (InitializeCounters, SetBoundaryDefinition, SetNormalizationInterval) are stateless and idempotent; NGHE Solution owns interpretation and consistency.
Sensor failure modes (drift, missing data, out-of-range values) can be detected and signaled via SafetyAlert without requiring external polling or synchronous queries.
open_risks: Sensor abstraction layer complexity: If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems implement sensor adapters inconsistently, counter logic may receive malformed or inconsistent data. Mitigation: Formalize sensor interface contract with strict validation and error handling in NGHE Solution.

Counter initialization race conditions: If Tasking commands and Vehicle/Teleoperation mode changes occur concurrently, counter state may become inconsistent. Mitigation: Enforce Tasking as stateless and idempotent; NGHE Solution must handle concurrent initialization requests safely.

SafetyAlert latency: If counter inconsistency or sensor failure detection is delayed, safety-critical alerts may not reach Safety Services in time. Mitigation: Define latency requirements for SafetyAlert events and implement real-time monitoring in FR3.4.

Telemetry subscription bottleneck: If multiple consumers (Teleoperation, external monitoring, logging) subscribe to Telemetry at high frequency, NGHE Solution may become a bottleneck. Mitigation: Implement rate-limiting and filtering in Telemetry interface; consider asynchronous publish-subscribe pattern.

Counter divergence detection sensitivity: If input/output counter divergence thresholds are set too low, false positives may flood SafetyAlert; if too high, real sensor failures may be missed. Mitigation: Calibrate thresholds based on sensor accuracy specifications and material flow variability; include in acceptance testing.
evidence_needed: Sensor abstraction interface specification (MaterialFlowSensor contract) with data types, error codes, and validation rules.
Counter state machine design showing initialization, accumulation, consistency checking, and failure detection transitions.
SafetyAlert event schema and latency requirements (e.g., anomaly detection must trigger alert within X milliseconds).
Telemetry subscription model specification (filtering, rate-limiting, buffering strategy).
Tasking interface command specification (InitializeCounters, SetBoundaryDefinition, SetNormalizationInterval) with idempotency guarantees and error handling.
Sensor failure mode analysis (FMEA or similar) identifying detectable failure modes and corresponding SafetyAlert signals.
Acceptance test plan covering: steady-state flow, blockage, sensor failure, counter divergence, concurrent Tasking commands, and Telemetry subscription under load.
Safety case evidence linking counter consistency detection (FR3.4) to worksite safety plan requirements and demonstrating that anomalies are reported with sufficient latency and reliability.
